column27: LLM's are a tool. It is how they are used that determines whether they are a force for good or not. This is dependent upon individual scenarios and a broad statement cannot be made. column26: They help those with disabilities with independence. They can speed up some manual tasks and answer general questions faster than a search engine. They are also good for brainstorming and summarizing. My personal favorite use of LLMs is handwriting to latex translation of complex formulas which is almost certainly a good thing.
column27: They allow blatant academic dishonesty that is easy to suspect but is often very hard to prove. They can provide a replacement for human relationships for those most at risk of shying away from people in times of need. They currently create so much content that is designed to be addictive and is faster to produce than human generated content for the sole purpose of making someone a few bucks.

column10: General questions
column26: They're convenient for finding some kinds of information quickly.
column27:
It's really hard to make paper writing assignments fair. It's hard to tell who is cheating and who is not and it's hard to reward students who are doing the reading for themselves. It's really, really hard to prove that a student is using an LLM even if you are basically sure that they have. 

There are many situations in which LLMs are convenient and even helpful if you are getting them to do something that you could already do for yourself and that you have a good grasp of. But if someone doesn't already have these skills, the LLM does a mediocre job and the person using it cannot tell. 

I think I'd think this regardless of my faith though, I think LLMs are a real problem for the education system if you don't want people to cheat or if you want them to learn how to do things themselves.
